title: Practice Questions Lectures 1 2 With Answers
body:
1. An investor is considering the following portfolios. He does not have access to the risk-free rate. Which portfolios cannot be on the efficient frontier? Portfolio Expected return Volatility A 25 % 30% B 22 % 35 % C 15 % 22 % D 18 % 20% E 15 % 13 % Choose only one of the following answers:

a. C, D, A b. B, C c. B, C, A d. Only B e. None of the above.

2. Which of the following statements is false?

a. According to the CAPM, all securities with a standard deviation greater than the market should provide a higher return than the market. b. The book to market ratio is proportional to the price to book ratio. c. A risky portfolio cannot have a beta of zero. d. Fama-French 3 factor model explains well the expected return of portfolios sorted on average historical returns. e. None of the above since they are all true.

3. According to the Capital Market Line, point A on the chart represents:

a. The risk-free asset.   b. The market portfolio. c. The portfolio that maximizes beta. d. The portfolio that minimizes the Sharpe ratio. e. A portfolio that contains equities and risk-free assets.

4. What does point e) on the graph represent?

a. The expected return on Portfolio A. b. The risk associated with the riskiest asset in Portfolio A. c. The return on risk. d. The risk premium of Portfolio A. e. The Sharpe ratio of Portfolio A.

5. You are provided with the following information. All stocks have 0 alphas and all measures are in annual units.

Security A B C Expected Excess 24.7 % 15.03% ? return Beta 2.3 1.4 1.1

What is the expected excess return on security C according to the CAPM?

a. 11.80% b. 13.0%   c. 9.6% d. 4.0% e. None of the above

Answer: We first need to infer the market premium. Take A, 𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 = 𝛽𝛽𝐴𝐴 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 , thus we have E[𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ] = E[𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 ]/ 𝛽𝛽𝐴𝐴 = 10.73. Then multiply it by 1.1 to get 11.80%.

6. According to Fama-French, which of the following statements is true? All other things being equal, a stock is likely to .

a. Have higher expected excess return than the CAPM predicts if its market-tobook value ratio (MV/BV or price-to-book ratio) is higher than that of the industry. b. Have higher expected excess return than the CAPM predicts if its MV/BV ratio is lower than the industry average. c. Have expected excess return equal to what the CAPM predicts if its MV/BV ratio is greater than that of the industry. d. Have expected return unrelated to its MV/BV ratio.

Answer: According to FF, higher book to market (low MV/BV) stocks have higher returns than what the market model/CAPM predicts.

7. Which of the following statements is true?

a. If two securities have a positive correlation coefficient, diversification between these two securities cannot be achieved. b. If two securities have a high standard deviation, then the portfolio composed of these two securities will necessarily also have a high standard deviation. c. It is impossible for the standard deviation of a portfolio composed of 2 securities to be less than the individual standard deviation of the two securities. d. The addition of one security to a portfolio may increase the systematic risk of the portfolio. e. The systematic risk of a single security with a beta of 0.5 is always greater than the systematic risk of a portfolio of 500 randomly selected securities.

8. Which of the following statements is true?

a. The point of minimum variance on the efficient frontier is the point where the Capital Market Line (CML) touches the efficient frontier. b. The X-axis of the Security Market Line (SML) measuring the risk is represented by the standard deviation   c. The CAPM is implied by the SML. d. The single index model for stocks sorted on beta holds well empirically. e. None of the above. They are all false.

9. Which of these is not a benefit of investing in common shares in Australia?

a. Consistently higher expected returns b. High liquidity. c. Relatively low transaction costs (buying and selling) d. Right to vote e. None of the above

10. In a rapidly rising market, a stock with a beta of 0.5 and 0 alpha is expected, relative to the market performance, to .

a. Move in the same direction as the market and earn a higher return than the market b. Move in the opposite direction of the market and earn a return that is lower (more negative) than the negative of the market return c. Move in the same direction as the market and earn a lower return than the market d. Move in the opposite direction of the market and earn a return that is higher (less negative) than the negative of the market return

A low beta means the stock will move by less than the market if the market moves upward. The positive beta means it will move in the same direction as the market.

11. Which of the following portfolios will be least preferred by a rational risk-averse investor? a. Portfolio A having excess return on investment as 10% and variance of 2%. b. Portfolio B having excess return on investment as 10% and variance of 4%. c. Portfolio C having excess return on investment as 14% and variance of 4%. d. Portfolio D having excess return on investment as 18% and variance of 6%.

Answer is B A risk-averse investor will prefer the portfolio that has the highest Sharpe ratio. However, Portfolio B has the lowest Sharpe ratio of all.   12. According to the Markowitz portfolio theory, the optimal risky portfolio is a portfolio: a. that carries the highest expected excess return to volatility ratio among all possible portfolios made of risky and risk-free assets b. that carries the least amount of risk. It is the global minimum variance portfolio. c. that carries the highest expected excess return among all portfolios on the Markowitz efficient frontier. d. that carries the lowest expected excess return to volatility ratio among all possible portfolios made of risky assets

Answer A The optimal risky portfolio carries the most reward in return per unit of risk taken.

13. Jessica is considering investing in two assets, Y and Z, with betas 2.1 and 1.6, respectively. Her broker tells her that the total return on the market portfolio will be 14% and that the risk-free rate is 6% over the next year. Moreover, the expected return on both assets is forecasted to be 20% for the next year based on various analyst forecasts. Which of the 2 stocks she should most likely invest in if analyst forecasts are precise: a. Asset Z. b. Asset Y. c. Neither of the assets. d. Both assets

Answer A.

Required return on Asset Y = 0.06 + [2.1 × (0.14 – 0.06)] = 22.8%

The required return on Asset Y (22.8%) is more than the expected return (20%). Therefore, Jessica should not invest in it. It means that it will experience a negative alpha.

Required return on Asset Z = 0.06 + [1.6 × (0.14 – 0.06)] = 18.8%

The required return on Asset Z (18.8%) is less than the expected return (20%). Therefore, Jessica should invest in it.   14. Susan has a portfolio whose standard deviation is estimated to be 11.68%. She is thinking of adding another asset to her portfolio whose standard deviation of returns is the same as her existing portfolio, but has a correlation coefficient with the existing portfolio of 0.65. If she adds the new asset to her portfolio, the standard deviation of the new portfolio will be: a. Equal to 11.68%. b. Less than 11.68%. c. More than 11.68%. d. Equal to the standard deviation of the added asset

Answer B Whenever the correlation coefficient is less than +1, diversification benefits occur and reduce the overall standard deviation of the portfolio.

15. A portfolio is invested equally between two assets. The variances of the two stocks are 4% and 8%, and the correlation between the two stocks is 0.3. The volatility of the combined portfolio is closest to: a. 19.62%. b. 16.25% c. 3.85%. d. 5.80%.

Answer A • σ2𝑝𝑝 = w𝐴𝐴2 σ𝐴𝐴2 + w2𝐵𝐵 σ2𝐵𝐵 + 2 𝑤𝑤𝐴𝐴 𝑤𝑤𝐵𝐵 σ𝐴𝐴 σ𝐵𝐵 ρ 𝐴𝐴,𝐵𝐵 =(0.5)^2(0.04)+(0.5)^2(0.08)+2(0.5)(0.5) (0.3)(√0.04) (√0.08) =0.0385 Vol is √0.0385 =

16. An investor gathered the following information regarding three stocks:

Expected Standard Stock Beta Return Deviation A 22% 25% 1.1 B 17% 30% 1.4 C 19% 23% 0.8

The return on the market portfolio is 15% and its volatility is 21%, and the risk-free rate of return is 4%.  Stock A’s non-systematic variance is closest to:

a. 0.0036 b. 0.0247 c. 0.0956 d. 0.0091

Answer D Idiosyncratic variance = (0.252) – (1.12 × 0.212) = 0.009139

17. If a stock lies above the SML, this would indicate that the stock: a. Is overvalued. b. Is undervalued. c. Has a beta above 1 d. Has a higher expected return than the market.

Answer B The stock is undervalued because the expected rate of return is higher than the required rate of return to compensate for its beta risk.

18. Select the answer below that gives the most precise response to the question.

A portfolio with 50% risky asset and 50% risk-free asset shall have: a. Half the standard deviation of the risky asset. b. Twice the returns of the risk-free asset. c. More standard deviation than the risky asset. d. Less standard deviation than the risk of the risk-free asset

Answer A The third choice is correct but less precise that answer A. The risk of an equally weighted risky and risk-free asset is always equal to 50% of the risk of the risky asset.

19. The CML's intercept on the y-axis is the:

a. Risk-free risk.   b. Market risk premium. c. Beta. d. Risk-free return.

Answer D The CML's intercept on the y-axis is the risk-free return.

20. According to the CAPM, in the presence of a risk-free asset, the market portfolio is most likely comprised of all: a. All assets and weighted based on equal weights. b. All assets and weighted based on market value weights. c. High Yield corporate bonds d. Only high-risk assets and weighted to maximize expected return for a given level of risk.

Answer is B The market portfolio is comprised of all assets and weighted according to their market value weights. It is the tangency portfolio. In equilibrium, if all investors have the same inputs, they shall have constructed the same efficient frontier. When choosing a portfolio to mix with the risk free asset, all investors will choose the same tangency portfolio. This portfolio becomes the market portfolio composed of all risk securities. The market value of each risky security thus represents the aggregate wealth of all investors. The weight of each security in the market portfolio is thus the market value of the company divided by the total market value of all stocks.

21. An investment manager forecasts that the stock market is going to decline. A client has specified that she wishes to remain invested in risky assets rather than holding a risk free asset. The investment manager is most likely to tilt the portfolio toward stocks with:

a. Betas of less than 1—he expects them to decline by less than the market. b. Betas of more than 1—they are expected to perform better than the market. c. Beta lower than the risk-free rate d. Betas of 1—they have perfect market exposure so they will not decline with the market.

Answer A. A beta of less than 1 means that the stock is defensive and will decline by less than the market. This can be seen from the CAPM: E(Rx)=Rf+β[E(Rm)−Rf]   22. The following information is available for the stock of Scranton Co.:

• The expected one-year holding period return is 25%. • Analysts’ forecast that the stock price is expected to be $37.50 in one year while it is currently valued at $30. • Dividend payout ratio = 0%. • The stock's covariance with the market is 0.08. • The standard deviation of the market portfolio is 15%. • The risk-free rate is 6%. • The expected market return is 10%.

All metrics are in annual units. If analysts’ forecast is right, then, the SML suggests, Scranton Co. is

A. Overvalued by $1.43 B. Undervalued by $1.43 C. Undervalued by $1.75 D. Overvalued by $1.75

Answer B.

The first choice is incorrect. Required rate of return is less than the expected rate of return.

Find the required rate of return:

Beta = .08/(.15)2 = 3.5556

CAPM/SML prediction = 6 + 3.5556 (10 – 6) = 20.2222%

Holding period return (HPR) = (37.50 – 30)/(30) = 25%

Alpha = HPR – CAPM/SML prediction = 25 – 20.22 = 4.78%

P0 × 4.78% = 30 × 4.78% = $1.43   23. Systematic risk: a. Can be decreased through diversification. b. Is the risk that is particular to a specific asset. c. Cannot be eliminated through diversification. d. Tend to increase during economic downturns

The second choice is incorrect. This describes unsystematic risk.

24. Which of the following portfolios is the most likely to have its total variance equal to the weighted average of the standard deviations of its constituent securities? a. Portfolio A: There is no correlation between the securities. b. Portfolio B: The prices of constituent securities move in opposite directions. c. Portfolio C: It gives absolutely no diversification benefits to the investor. d. Portfolio D: The average correlation between the securities is 0.5

When the securities are perfectly positively correlated, there will not be any diversification benefits and the total variance of the portfolio is equal to the weighted average of the variance of its constituents.

25. A stock's beta is equal to 0.87. The covariance of the stock's returns with the market returns is 0.027. The standard deviation of the stock returns is 21%. The stock's correlation with the market is equal to: a. 0.73. b. 0.13. c. 0.67. d. 0.18

Answer is A

Beta of the stock = βi = Cov (Ri,Rm) / σ2m.

Variance of market returns = σ2m = 0.027 / 0.87 = 0.03

Standard deviation of market returns = √0.03 = 0.18

Correlation of stock with market = Cov (Ri,Rm) / (σm × σi) = 0.027 / (0.18 × 0.21) = 0.73   26. If the market risk premium is 4%, the risk-free rate is 6%, and a stock has a beta of 1.1 and a standard deviation of 3% then, according to the CAPM, the expected return from the stock is: a. 10.4%. b. 11.0%. c. 15.0%. d. 17.0%

Answer is A Using the CAPM, the return is: Rx=Rf+β[E(Rm)−Rf]=6%+(1.1×4%)=10.4%

27. Which of the following is the least accurate description of an assumption of the CAPM? a. All investors have the same time horizon. b. Investors can borrow or lend at the risk-free rate. c. Investors are not able to correctly anticipate inflation. d. The market portfolio is efficient

CAPM makes the assumption that there is no inflation or change in interest rates, or any inflation is fully anticipated and reflected into prices and returns.

28. Which of the following three asset return measures is informative about the risk of an investor's portfolio returns? A. Return on the asset. B. Variance of the returns on the assets. C. Covariance of the asset returns with the risk free rate. D. The standard deviation of the risk free rate

Answer B. The return itself is not informative about the risk, but the variance of returns is.

29. Do an increase in correlation coefficients between different asset classes during a financial crisis provide diversification benefits to investors? A. Yes. Higher correlation results in more diversification benefits. B. Changes in correlation coefficients do not impact diversification benefits.   C. No. As correlation coefficients increase during a crisis, the diversification benefit is reduced because all assets tend to comove more with each others. D. Yes. A change in correlation will increase the specific risk of the portfolio.

The level of correlation coefficient and the effectiveness of diversification are negatively related. High correlation coefficients suggest low diversification benefits.

30. Total volatility of a stock or portfolio is best defined as the standard error of the residuals plus: A. total risk. B. automatic risk. C. variance of the market. D. systematic risk

Answer D Standard error of the residuals plus systematic risk (beta squared times market variance) is equal to total risk of the asset.

31. An analyst estimated a market model for IMB using excess returns and determined that the alpha was statistically significant. Then the analyst could most likely conclude that IMB: A. was undervalued. B. was overvalued. C. was correctly valuated D. performed abnormally

Answered D If an analyst finds a statistically significant alpha, this suggests that the stock is either undervalued (alpha>0) or overvalued (alpha<0). Given that we don t know the sign of alpha we can only conclude that the stock has abnormally performed.

32. Based on the data provided in the table below, which of the three assets (X, Y and Z) is the best choice for an investor who plans to invest all of his wealth in one asset only?   Expected Excess Return Standard Deviation Correlation with the Asset (Percentage) (Percentage) Market X 12 30 0.4 Y 12 25 0.5 Z 14 26 0.6 Market 13 26 1.0 Risk-free asset 5

a. Asset X. b. Asset Y. c. Asset Z. d. Impossible to answer

Answer C.

For single asset selection, we care about Sharpe ratio defined as the ratio of expected excess return over excess return standard deviation/volatility. Z offers the highest Sharpe ratio 0.12/0.25 = 0.4800

33. An investor wants to create a large and diversified portfolio. She wants to be within 1% range of market index volatility of 19.21% when doing so she wants to minimize transaction costs. Given the table below, how many stocks does she need in her portfolio to attain her objectives?

a. 200 and more stocks. b. About 100 stocks.   c. About 50 stocks. d. At least 1000 stocks

19.21%+1% = 20.21%. She wants to minimize transaction costs, so she wants to trade the least number of stocks possible. From the table, a portfolio of 50 stocks has an volatility of 20.20% which is below her upper bound of risk appetite of 20.21%.

34. An investor’s portfolio volatility becomes higher than it was before while not being compensated with a higher expected return when:

a. There is an increase in the portfolio beta. b. There is an increase in the portfolio total volatility without any increase in the non-systematic volatility. c. The increased in volatility is not explained by an increase in the non diversifiable risk. d. The increased in volatility is not explained by an increase in the diversifiable risk.

When the increase in the total volatility cannot be explained by an increase in the non diversifiable risk, it means that the increase in the total volatility is due to an increase in the diversifiable risk (increase in residual volatility/idiosyncratic risk). Unlike market risk, an investor is not compensated for the diversifiable risk because it can be diversified away.

35. An investor is analyzing the returns on a portfolio of two stocks, Alpha Ltd. (AL) and Beta Ltd. (BL). The following Fama-French model results are estimated:

• 𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 = 0.020 + 3 × 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 − 6 × 𝑅𝑅SMB,𝑡𝑡 + 0 × 𝑅𝑅HML,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 • 𝑅𝑅𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡 = 0.015 + 6 × 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 + 3 × 𝑅𝑅SMB,𝑡𝑡 + 0 × 𝑅𝑅HML,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡

where all returns are in annual units and in excess of the risk-free rate. Thirty percent of the portfolio is invested in AL, and 70% is invested in BL. What is the beta exposure of the portfolio to the Small minus Big factor?

a. -3.30. b. 0.35.   c. Hard to say given available information. d. 5.1

Answer b.

• Portfolio SMB beta is 0.3*(-6) + 0.7*(3) = 0.35

36. An investor is analyzing the returns on a portfolio of two stocks, Alpha Ltd. (AL) and Beta Ltd. (BL). The following Fama-French model results are estimated:

• 𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 = 0.020 + 3 × 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 − 6 × 𝑅𝑅SMB,𝑡𝑡 + 0 × 𝑅𝑅HML,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 • 𝑅𝑅𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡 = 0.015 + 6 × 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 + 3 × 𝑅𝑅SMB,𝑡𝑡 + 0 × 𝑅𝑅HML,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡

where all returns are in annual units and in excess of the risk-free rate. What are the percentages that need to be invested in AL and BL to make the portfolio of the two stocks neutral to the Small minus Big factor?

a. AL: 45.15% and BL: 54.85%. b. AL: 43.22% and BL: 56.78%. c. Hard to say given available information. d. AL: 33.33% and BL: 66.67%.

Answer d.

• Let us denote by w the weight invested in AL and 1-w the weight invested in BL • Portfolio SMB beta is: • w*(-6)+(1-w)*(3) = 0 • w*(-9) = -3 • w = 3/9 = 33.33%

37. An investor is analyzing the returns on a portfolio of two stocks, Alpha Ltd. (AL) and Beta Ltd. (BL). The following Fama-French model results have been estimated from historical data:

• 𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 = 0.020 + 3 × 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 − 6 × 𝑅𝑅SMB,𝑡𝑡 + 0 × 𝑅𝑅HML,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 • 𝑅𝑅𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡 = 0.015 + 6 × 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 + 3 × 𝑅𝑅SMB,𝑡𝑡 + 0 × 𝑅𝑅HML,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡

where all returns are in annual units and in excess of the risk-free rate. The expected excess return on the market is -10%, and for the SMB factor it is -2% over the next year due to bad economic forecasts. Moreover, you do not think any of the two stocks will incur an abnormal performance over the next year. What is your best forecast of the  expected excess returns of the market neutral portfolio of the two stocks over the next year?

Answer: 30%

We first need to find the weight that makes the portfolio of the two stocks market neutral:

• Let us denote by w the weight invested in AL and 1-w the weight invested in BL • Portfolio market beta is: • w*(3)+(1-w)*(6) = 0 • w*(-3) = -6 • w = 2 = 200 % in AL and -100% (we short) BL

Now we can compute the SML beta of the market neutral portfolio:

• SMB beta: 2*(-6)+(-1)*(3) = -15 • Expected excess return = -15 * -2% = 30% (no alpha to be taken into account as you believe the two stocks will both have 0 abnormal return over the next year.

38. Antonio derives the following two-factor model to explain the returns on the stock of Global Traders (GT):

• 𝑅𝑅𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺,𝑡𝑡 = 𝛼𝛼𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺 + 2.5 × 𝐹𝐹𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺,𝑡𝑡 − 1.5 × 𝐹𝐹Inflation,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺,𝑡𝑡

The following information (all in annual units) is also available

• The expected excess return forecast for the stock last year was 13% (including alpha) • Over the last year, the value of gross domestic product (GDP) was 1.5% lower than its expected value, while inflation was 2% higher. • Company-idiosyncratic return (i.e., unrelated to GDP and the inflation rate) was 1.25%.

Given the information above, what is your best estimate of the realized excess return on the company's stock for last year?

a. 15% b. 13.50% c. 7.50% d. 13%

• Realized Excess return = 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐺𝐺𝐺𝐺,𝑡𝑡 ] + Deviation from expected return • Deviation from expected return = 2.5 × (−0.015) – (1.5 × 0.02) + 0.0125 = -5.50% • Realized Excess return = 13 - 5.50% = 7.50%

39. After graduating from a Bachelor specialization in finance, you moved to New York and work for a quantitative investment fund named Q-Investment. This fund specializes in construction and investment in zero-beta strategies. Currently, QInvestment is looking to create new strategies and has hired you for developing new investment ideas. The management at Q-Investment has recently spotted a new exchange traded fund (ETF) named SC 50 which consists of 50 small market capitalization companies (Small Cap). Q-Investment thinks that this ETF offers an attractive investment opportunity. Your boss at Q-Investment has asked you to build a new strategy zero-beta with positive returns on average that would combine a position in SC 50 and in an ETF replicating the market index S&P 500.

For your analysis you adopt the single index model and your first instinct is to calculate the beta SC 50 relative to the market index S & P500. You conclude that 50 SC excess returns follow:

𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 = 𝑎𝑎𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 + 𝛽𝛽𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀 + 𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50

where 𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 =𝑟𝑟𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 −𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 , 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀 =𝑟𝑟𝑀𝑀 −𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 , and the T-Bill rate (𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 ) is currently 3%. Your study shows that SC 50 is not a completely diversified fund and thus its returns are influenced by a specific risk 𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50,𝑡𝑡 which is on average 0 (E[𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50,𝑡𝑡 ]=0) with variance of 2%. Your assistant at Q-Investment sends his latest forecast for SC 50 and the S & P 500. The expected return of SC50 (𝑟𝑟𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50,𝑡𝑡 ) is 17%. The expected return of the S&P 500 (𝑟𝑟𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ) is 7%. The variance of the market is 11% and the covariance between SC 50 and the S&P 500 is 22%. Given these information answer the following questions. Note that all rates, variances, and covariance are expressed on an annual basis.

a. What is the variance of SC 50? b. What is the Sharpe Ratio of SC 50 and of the market index? If you could only invest in one of the ETFs (i.e. SC 50 or S&P500), which one would you choose? c. Calculate the required return of SC 50 consistent with CAPM. Is SC 50 having abnormal return? Calculate SC 50 alpha (𝑎𝑎𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 ) relative to the performance required under the CAPM. Is SC 50 over- or underperforming the market?

Your objective is now to build a zero-beta portfolio. To do so, you decide first to construct a portfolio with unit beta of SC 50 and the T-bill. You will then trade that portfolio against the ETF which has a unit market beta by construction.

d. Build a unit-beta portfolio that combines a position in SC 50 and the T-bill. What are the weights w and 1-w to invest in SC 50 and the T-bill in order for the combined portfolio to have unit-beta. What is the interpretation/exposure of our portfolio in terms of market risk and specific risk.   e. Based on your answer to d), describe a zero-beta strategy that would allow you to generate profits on average. Please explain your strategy, give the annual excess return generated by your strategy. What positions does it require in SC 50, the T-bill, and the S&P500? f. What is the Sharpe Ratio of the strategy you proposed in the previous question? Compare this Sharpe ratio with the ratios calculated in a) and comment. Hint: To calculate the variance of your strategy, use the fact that your strategy is zero-beta by construction.

Answers:

a.

b.

c.

d.

We create rc = 𝑤𝑤 rSC 50 + (1 − 𝑤𝑤) rf so that βc = 𝑤𝑤 βSC 50 +(1 − 𝑤𝑤)βrf = 𝑤𝑤 βSC 50 =1

Given that βSC 50 = 2, we have 𝑤𝑤 = 0.5

Thus we need to invest 50% in SC 50 and 50% in the T-bill.  Then the return dynamics of this strategy is: 𝑅𝑅𝐶𝐶 = 0.5 𝑎𝑎𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 + 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀 + 0.5 𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50

In expectation, the excess return of this portfolio is 0.5 * 6% + 1 * 4% = 7%.

e.

We will take a long position in the positive alpha portfolio constructed in d) and short the ETF. The sum of the weights needs to 1 and because the combined portolio and the the ETF have the same beta, we can simply invest 100% in one and -100% in the other.

𝑅𝑅𝑍𝑍𝑍𝑍 = 𝑅𝑅𝐶𝐶 − 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀 = 0.5 𝑎𝑎𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 + 0.5 𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50

The weight invested in SC 500 is thus 50% (100% times 50%)

The weight invested in T-bill is thus 50% (100% times 50%)

The weight invested in the ETF is -100%.

In expectation, the excess return of this portfolio is 0.5 * 6% + 1 * 4% = 7%.

f.

The variance of the portfolio is: V(0.5 𝑎𝑎𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 + 0.5 𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 ) = 𝑉𝑉(0.5 𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 )=0.52 V(𝜀𝜀𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆50 ) = 0.0050

The volatility is thus 7.07%

The Sharpe ratio is 7%/7.07% = 1.

40. If a stock has a monthly excess return of 0.58% and a monthly volatility of 12.91%. What is the Sharpe ratio of the stock in annual units?

Answer:

Annual expected excess return is 0.58% times 12 = 7%

Annual volatility is 12.91% times square root of 12 = 20%

The annual Sharpe ratio is 35%

41. Which statement is true about the momentum strategy?   a. It involves sorting stocks on past average returns by taking a long position in stocks with low historical performance and short stocks with high historical performance. b. The turnover of the momentum strategy is comparable to a passive buy-andhold portfolio. c. The annual alpha of the momentum strategy can be as high as 17% d. There is little empirical evidence that the momentum strategy has historically experienced very large and negative returns.

42. Which statement is true about the volatility-managed strategies?

a. It is based on the theory that the optimal portfolio weight of a meanvariance investor is proportional to the variance of the risky asset. b. It does not perform very well for betting-against-betta investment strategies. c. During period of high uncertainty, it increases the weight of the risk asset and decreases the weight invested in the risk-free ones. d. The volatility-managed portfolio is relatively simple to implement and only requires an estimate of the portfolio’s volatility. e. The volatility-managed portfolio applies to a market index ETF will induce more transaction cost than implementing the momentum strategy.

43. You have been hired as a portfolio manager assistant by an investment company named Alpha2 Inc. The portfolio manager you are assisting is interested in the common stocks of two companies: ABC Inc (ABC) and CDE Inc (CDE). He would like to combine these two stocks in a portfolio for the purpose of structuring a new investment product for her clients. Below are the information in annual units you gathered regarding the two stocks and the market: ABC Stock: Expected excess return (𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 ]) 12% Market beta (𝛽𝛽𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑀𝑀 ) 1.3 SMB beta (𝛽𝛽𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 ) 1.6 HML beta (𝛽𝛽𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 ) -0.8 CDE Stock: Expected excess return (𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶,𝑡𝑡 ]) 6% Market beta (𝛽𝛽𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶,𝑀𝑀 ) 0.8 SMB beta (𝛽𝛽𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶,𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 ) 0.7 HML beta (𝛽𝛽𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶,𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 ) -1.5  Other information: Market risk premium (E[R M,t ] ) 7% Risk free (𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 ) 3% Note that SMB and HML are two risk factors in addition to the market. In other words, assume that the two company excess returns (𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 = 𝑟𝑟𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 − 𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 ) satisfy

𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 = 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑀𝑀 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡

where j is either ABC or CDE, and 𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 and 𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 are the return on the factors. Moreover, on average, we have

𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 ] = 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑀𝑀 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ] + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ].

A. Given that Fama-French 3-factor model holds perfectly for both ABC and CDE, what is the risk premia for 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] and 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ]?

Note: If you did not know how to answer the question A) assume that 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] = 2% and 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] = 0.5% to answer the following questions.

B. Another stock, FGH is also traded and has an average annual excess return of 12% with betas to the market, SMB and HML equal to 0.7, 1.2 et -0.9, respectively. According to the 3-factor model, is FGH correctly valued?

Answers:

A. We need to solve a system of 2 equations with 2 unknowns. There is different ways to do that, and the way the answer is designed is to leave ABC unchanged but multiply the factor representation of CDE by the right number to hedge away either SML or HML when combining ABC + CDE.

We first need to remove the market premium from ABC and CDE expected excess return:

𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 ] = 1.3 𝐸𝐸�𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 � + 1.6 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −0.8 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶,𝑡𝑡 ] = 0.8 𝐸𝐸�𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 � + 0.7 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −1.5 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ]

𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 ] − 1.3 𝐸𝐸�𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 � = 1.6 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −0.8 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶,𝑡𝑡 ] − 0.8 𝐸𝐸�𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 � = 0.7 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −1.5 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ]

2.90% = 1.6 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −0.8 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] 0.40% = 0.7 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −1.5 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ]  Now, if we want to infer HML we just need to multiply the representation above for CDE by the negative of the ratio of the SMB betas so that to have a portfolio SMB hedged. To this end we need the multiplication factor applied to CDE is -1.6/0.7.

Then ABC + (-1.6/0.7) CDE becomes

1.6 1.6 2.90% − 0.40% = 0 + (−0.8 + 1.5 )𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] 0.7 0.7

Which allows us to conclude that 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] = 0.76%.

To infer SMB, we need to multiply CDE by 0.8/(-1.5), then do ABC + 0.8/(-1.5) CDE and work out the math just like what we did for HML. You will get 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] = 2.19%.

B. Given the FF factor representation assuming 0 alpha, FGH expected excess return should be

𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹,𝑡𝑡 ] = 0.7 𝐸𝐸�𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 � + 1.2 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆𝑆,𝑡𝑡 ] + −0.9 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻𝐻,𝑡𝑡 ] = 6.85%

Thus, FGH as an alpha of 12% - 6.85% = 5.15% and is not correctly priced by the 3-factor model.

44. You have been hired as a portfolio manager assistant by an investment company named Alpha2 Inc. The portfolio manager you are assisting is interested in the volatility-managed portfolio of ETF A (A) and ETF B (B). He would like to volatility managed these two stocks for the purpose of structuring a new investment product for her clients. Below are the information you gathered regarding the two stocks and the market: ETF A: Expected excess return (𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐴𝐴,𝑡𝑡 ]) 12% Alpha (𝛼𝛼𝐴𝐴 ) 2% Total Variance 4% ETF B: Expected excess return (𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝐵𝐵,𝑡𝑡 ]) 8% Alpha (𝛼𝛼𝐵𝐵 ) 3% Total Variance 16% Other information:  Market risk premium (E[𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ] ) 7% Risk free (𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 ) 3% Here we adopt a one-factor index model that the two ETF excess returns (𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 = 𝑟𝑟𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 − 𝑟𝑟𝑓𝑓 ) so that

𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 = 𝛼𝛼𝑗𝑗 + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑀𝑀 𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡

where j is either A or B. Moreover, on average, we have

𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 ] = 𝛼𝛼𝑗𝑗 + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑀𝑀 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ].

A. Given the information above, what must be the market betas of the 2 ETFs? B. Accounting for systematic risk exposure, which ETF would you rather invest in?

C. Would you answer in B be the same if you were to volatility-managed each ETF? Which one would you pick?

Answers:

A. We can play around the factor representation of any of the two ETFs to infer their betas using

(𝐸𝐸�𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 � − 𝛼𝛼𝑗𝑗 )/𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ] = 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑀𝑀

We get 1 for ETF A and 0.5 for ETF B.

B. We would choose ETF B as its alpha is higher than ETF A. C. Volatility managed ETFs are obtained by multiplying their excess return representation by the ratio of 1 over their respective volatility. We get 1 1 1 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑗𝑗,𝑡𝑡 ] = 𝛼𝛼𝑗𝑗 + 𝛽𝛽𝑗𝑗,𝑀𝑀 𝐸𝐸[𝑅𝑅𝑀𝑀,𝑡𝑡 ]. 𝜎𝜎𝑗𝑗 𝜎𝜎𝑗𝑗 𝜎𝜎𝑗𝑗 1 1 For ETF A we have 𝛼𝛼 equal to 10% and for ETF B we have 𝛼𝛼𝑗𝑗 equal to 𝜎𝜎𝑗𝑗 𝑗𝑗 𝜎𝜎𝑗𝑗 7.5%. After volatility managing the ETFs, we would choose ETF A instead of ETF B after accounting for systematic risk exposure.
